info.title: getting started
info.text: In this demo we'll use GANSynth a generative model by Google Magenta to synthesize musical instrument sounds (ie "timbre") entirely in the browser. GANSynth uses a GAN (Generative Adversarial Network) trained on audio spectrograms of acoustic instruments. Rather than playing back recorded samples, it generates new audio textures from scratch, each with a randomly generated timbre.

info.title: libraries
info.text: We import five libraries. nn handles the DOM work. Tone.js manages the Web Audio API and lets us play back audio buffers. TensorFlow.js powers the model's inference. The two Magenta music imports, core.js and gansynth.js, give us the gansynth global with the GANSynth class.

info.title: global vars
info.text: We'll create three global variables, model will hold the GANSynth instance, button is the play button (kept as a reference so we can update its label during synthesis), and list is the note selector dropdown.

info.title: setup: model and UI
info.text: In setup (which runs on page load) we create the GANSynth model by pointing it at a checkpoint URL, this is a folder of pre-trained weights on Google's servers. The model isn't downloaded yet at this point, it loads later when first used. We also create a select dropdown and a button.

info.title: nn.notes
info.text: The dropdown is populated with nn.notes, a pre-defined array in the nn library containing all 12 note names ('C', 'C#', 'D', etc). This gives the user a way to choose a pitch for the synthesized sound.

info.title: Tone.start and lazy init
info.text: The playGANSynth function handles synthesis and playback. We first update the button label as feedback. Then await Tone.start() resumes the browser's AudioContext, browsers suspend audio until a user interaction, so this must be called inside a click handler. If the model hasn't been initialized yet, we initialize it now.

info.title: generating audio
info.text: We convert the selected note name to a frequency using nn.noteToFrequency(list.value + 4), the + 4 appends an octave number. Then we convert that to a MIDI note (which the model expects) using nn.frequencyToMidi(freq). Then we generate a spectorgram for that pitch with a randomly chosen timbre model.randomSample(note), every call produces a different instrument-like texture.

info.title: specgram to audio
info.text: The model works in the frequency domain (spectrograms), so we need to convert its output to actual audio samples. To do this we call model.specgramsToAudio(spec). We set the sr (sample rate) to 16000 Hz, that's the rate GANSynth was trained at. We then wrap the samples in a Tone.js audio buffer using the Web Audio API's createBuffer.

info.title: playing the buffer
info.text: We create a Tone.BufferSource connect it to the default audio output with .toDestination(), and call .start() to play it. Once the button label is restored the function is done. Each click generates and plays an entirely new sound.

info.title: lots more
info.text: GANSynth can do more than random timbres, the model works in a continuous latent space, so you can interpolate between two points to smoothly morph one timbre into another. The Magenta team's GANSynth blog post and magenta-js documentation cover the full API, including sampleZVectors for exploring the latent space directly.
